coredata.dc:title: Solid-state carbon dots with orange phosphorescence and tunable fluorescence via in-situ growth in phthalimide crystal matrix
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fanyong
Yi, Chunhui
Hao, Zhonghua
Wang, Yao
Xu, Ming
Zhou, Keqing
Shi, Fangyu
Xu, Jinxia
coredata.dc:description: Solid-state fluorescent and phosphorescent carbon dots (CDs) have widespread applications, with difficulties in obtainment. In this study, solid-state carbon dots (SCDs) emitting green, yellow, and orange fluorescence were fabricated using urea and phthalic acid as precursors. The band gap size was systematically controlled by varying the ratio of raw material nitrogen. By changing the temperature during synthesis, the phosphorescent properties of SCDs were adjusted, and the longest lifetime possessed 156.8 ms of orange phosphorescence. CDs were attached to the crystals of phthalimide, allowing a certain spacing between the particles and suppressing the phenomenon of aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ), thus obtaining solid-state fluorescence. Detailed characterization and density flooding theory (DFT) calculations further indicate that the reduction of the conjugation domain leads to the redshift of the solid-state fluorescence. The phosphorescence appearance is attributed to the presence of crystals that inhibit the nonradiative relaxation channels, and N elements that promote spin-orbit coupling interactions. Based on their special luminescent properties, they have been successfully applied in the fabrication of (0.38,0.54), (0.44,0.54) and (0.51, 0.47) light-emitting diodes, as well as in the production of secure anti-counterfeit cryptographic inks and their successful application in digital encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid-state tunable fluorescence
Light-emitting diodes
Phosphorescence
Encryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) SEM images of G-, Y-, and O –SCDs; (d–f) TEM images of G-, Y-, and O-SCDs; (g–i) TEM images of CDs (insets are HRTEM images).
(a) FTIR; (b) XRD; (c) UV-Vis spectra (d) Raman spectra of Phthalimide,G-SCDs, Y -SCDs and O -SCDs.
(a–c) PL emission and (d-f) PL excitation spectra of G -SCDs, Y-SCDs, O-SCDs. (g-i) Excitation-emission matrix of G -SCDs, Y-SCDs, O-SCDs.
LUMO and HOMO states of the established model.
(a–c) Fluorescence emission spectra of G-, Y-, O-SCDs-based LEDs (insets are Fluorescent images); (d-f) CIE chromaticity coordinates.

(a) Normalized RTP emission spectra of SCDs at 160, 180, 200 °C; (b) RTP decay spectra of SCDs-160, 180, and 200 powders in (c) Jablonski plots of the tertiary system of CDs generating the RTP pathway (d) Photographs of SCDs-160, 180 powders at different delay times after turning off the UV excitation light (e) encrypted images using SCDs-160.

head.ce:title: Solid-state carbon dots with orange phosphorescence and tunable fluorescence via in-situ growth in phthalimide crystal matrix

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes Tianjin 300387, PR China)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tiangong University, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tiangong University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solid-state fluorescent and phosphorescent carbon dots (CDs) have widespread applications, with difficulties in obtainment. In this study, solid-state carbon dots (SCDs) emitting green, yellow, and orange fluorescence were fabricated using urea and phthalic acid as precursors. The band gap size was systematically controlled by varying the ratio of raw material nitrogen. By changing the temperature during synthesis, the phosphorescent properties of SCDs were adjusted, and the longest lifetime possessed 156.8 ms of orange phosphorescence. CDs were attached to the crystals of phthalimide, allowing a certain spacing between the particles and suppressing the phenomenon of aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ), thus obtaining solid-state fluorescence. Detailed characterization and density flooding theory (DFT) calculations further indicate that the reduction of the conjugation domain leads to the redshift of the solid-state fluorescence. The phosphorescence appearance is attributed to the presence of crystals that inhibit the nonradiative relaxation channels, and N elements that promote spin-orbit coupling interactions. Based on their special luminescent properties, they have been successfully applied in the fabrication of (0.38,0.54), (0.44,0.54) and (0.51, 0.47) light-emitting diodes, as well as in the production of secure anti-counterfeit cryptographic inks and their successful application in digital encryption.

Synthesis and mechanism of SCDs emitting solid-state fluorescence and phosphorescence.
ga1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a material with a variety of special properties, including green and widely available raw materials, excellent optical properties and good biocompatibility, have emerged as popular topic for scholars in many related fields [1–4]. In the majority of studies, the solid state of CDs is less frequently investigated than the liquid state. Most of the CDs are subject to this aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ) [5,6]. It is attributed to an excess of direct Π-Π interactions and fluorescence (FRET) [7]. In this way, SCDs cannot achieve the same PL behavior as their solutions. For the purpose of obtaining SCDs, portion of scholars mixed CDs with other matrices, such as inorganic salts, polymers such as starch, nanofibers and polyethylene glycol [8–10]. The fluorescence of SCDs made by this method is retained through uniform dispersion and spatial confinement since the substrate acts as a medium such as water. The preparation process is usually complex and costly, and there are ACQ problems at high loading rates. It would be better to be able to synthesize CDs with solid-state fluorescent properties in one step [11]. The pathway to synthesize long-wavelength SCDs and SCDs with fluorescence properties that can be tuned is still at an early stage, where few approaches are available. Therefore, it remains necessary to develop a simple and economical method to prepare powders with tunable fluorescent CDs and to investigate their mechanism.

The development of CDs with room-temperature phosphorescence effect, reported in recent years, has greatly facilitated their application. Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials have attracted extensive research in the construction of optical safety devices due to their unique triplet state process and afterglow luminescence [12]. The observation of phosphorescence is in principle very difficult because it involves spin forbidden jumps, which lead to a low probability of intersystem crossing (ISC), and radiation paths between single and triple states [13]. The current materials used to obtain phosphorescence are mostly pure organic materials or organometallic materials, but these materials have the disadvantages of being expensive, toxic, with potential environmental hazards and bad phosphorescence properties [14,15]. On the contrary, RTP CDs materials have the advantages of low toxicity, lower cost and more stable optical properties. Phosphorescent CDs have been studied to emit phosphorescence under water dispersion with a solution lifetime of up to 687 ms, and some scholars have synthesized solid green RTP CDs with an afterglow of up to 5 s or more. All these make the research of RTP CDs a hot topic [16,17]. Typical methods to gain RTP CDs include two-step mixing of CDs with substrates such as polyurethane, filter paper, silica, and zeolite to obtain them [18–20]. The presence of these substrates provides a rigid backbone and forms hydrogen bonds to inhibit the dissipation of molecular vibrations, and also prevents the penetration of oxygen molecules, water and high temperatures, thus avoiding their triplet quenching. Another method is to derive RTP CDs by adding halogens, carbonyl groups, siloxyl groups and nitrogen elements in one step. It effectively promotes ISC to obtain phosphorescence [21,22]. Long-wavelength phosphorescence has the advantage of being more resistant to background interference and transmitting signals farther than ordinary green phosphorescence, bringing new colors and forms to the encrypted display of information [16,23]. But no matter how to get RTP CDs, most of the RTPs of CDs today are still in the short wavelength range such as green, and there are few studies on RTPs at long wavelengths, which limits the application of CDs in the field of secure encryption. The development of a synthetic simple CDs capable of emitting long-wavelength phosphorescence is imminent.

Herein, solid-state fluorescence-emitting and room-temperature phosphorescence-emitting SCDs were synthesized by a one-pot solvothermal method using urea (UA) and phthalic acid (PA) as precursors. CDs with green, yellow and orange solid-state fluorescence were obtained by adjusting the ratio of urea and PA in the precursors. Phthalimide as a substrate for CDs was confirmed by electron microscopy and infrared spectroscopy, and XPS energy spectrum. The structure of SCDs was simulated in Gaussian software, and the relationship between energy gap change and fluorescence redshift was confirmed by DFT theory. And it is also successfully applied to the production of color LEDs. The temperature of the synthesis process was adjusted to obtain SCDs with orange phosphorescent properties, and the effects of nitrogen, crystal and temperature on phosphorescent emission were explored. Based on their unique orange afterglow, SCDs can make anti-counterfeit inks and participate in digital anti-counterfeit encryption applications. Likewise, this study opens up novel ideas for the facile synthesis of self-quenching resistant solid-state luminescent and phosphorescent emitting CDs, and further explores the wide application of CDs in the field of light-emitting diodes and anti-counterfeit encryption.

Urea (UA > 99.5 %) and phthalic acid (PD, 98 %) were purchased from Tianjin Windship Chemical Reagent Co. Ethanol (> 99.5 %) was obtained from Tianjin Huihang Chemical Technology Co. N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and ethyl acetate (EA) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). It was used without further purification.

It was performed by Regulus 8100 cold field emission scanning electron microscope and JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope to obtain the morphology of SCDs. Fluorescence spectra were obtained by a Shimadzu fluorescence spectrophotometer. The FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer was used to acquire fluorescence and phosphorescence decay curves. FT-IR is implemented using a FTIR-650 Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer. The Roman spectra were obtained using a Horiba Raman spectrometer (XploRA PLUS). The UV–vis spectra were obtained by TU-1901 UV-Visible spectrophotometer.

SCDs were prepared by adding UA and PA in different ratios to ethanol and using a hydrothermal method. Specifically, 0.6 g of UA and 0.83 g of PD (UA: PD = 2:1) were solubilized in the 30 mL of ethanol. After sonication for 20 min until the drugs were completely dissolved, the solution was transferred into a 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, warmed to 200 °C and maintained for 5 h. The solution was then transferred to a 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The precipitation obtained was collected by centrifugation after cooling to room temperature, washed repeatedly with ethanol and deionized water, and oven dried at 60 °C for 6 h to obtain orange SCDs (O-SCDs). The green and yellow SCDs were obtained by changing the ratio of precursors to 0.2:1 and 1:1 and repeating the above steps.

Phosphorescent-emitting SCDs-140, SCDs-160, and SCDs-180 were also synthesized by the solvent method. 0.6 g of UA and 0.83 g of PA were mixed completely in 30 mL of ethanol, transferred to a kettle, and maintained for 5 h after warming to 140, 160, and 180 °C, respectively, to obtain SCDs-140, SCDs-160, and SCDs-180. 180.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of green, yellow and orange LED Diode
ce:section.ce:para: The peak emission wavelength of commercial blue LED chips is concentrated at 365 nm, which is used as the excitation source for down-conversion LED. The SCDs powder was mixed thoroughly with the heat-curing resin, and the mixture was applied on the LED chip and finally cured with 0.5 mL of ethylenediamine for 4 h. Green, yellow and orange LEDs were obtained.

The ink was obtained by mixing propanetriol, polyvinylpyrrolidone, and ethanol and sonicating for 30 min 0.3 g SCDs-160 were mixed completely with 1 mL of ink to obtain an ink with cryptographic properties.

Multicolor SCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal mixtures of PA and UA in different ratios. in particular, orange SCDs (O-SCDs) of 599 nm were obtained when the UA: PD ratio was 2:1. The yellow SCDs (Y-SCDs) at 540 nm and the green SCDs (G-SCDs) at 520 nm were obtained with the UA: PD ratios of 1:1 and 0.2:1. When the N content continued to increase, SCDs also did not occur to continue the red shift, 2:1 for the maximum emission wavelength of SCDs is 599 nm, when the N content accounted for 11.6 % (Supporting Information, Fig. S10 and Table S1). The morphology, UV absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra and infrared spectra were characterized, and SCDs were found to be loaded on the crystals of phthalimide. The mechanism of color change of the three fluorescent SCDs was also investigated using Raman spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime.

The morphology of G-, Y- and O-SCDs was characterized by electron microscopy. The SEM results of G-, Y-, and O-SCDs are shown in Fig. 1a–c. The external morphology of G-, Y-, and O-SCDs shows elongated needle-like crystals with regular shape, and the length of G-, Y-, and O-SCDs gradually increases, but they all have similar external morphology with phthalimide (Fig. S5). The unground O-SCDs were subjected to single-crystal diffraction experiments, and the structural picture obtained proved that the crystals were o-phthalmic dipeptidyl imides (Fig. S6 and S7). In contrast to the structural data given by the Cambridge Data Center for pure phthalimides, there is only a slight difference between the data for O-SCDs and theirs, which is attributed to the fact that most of the CDs are not deeply loaded in the crystal cell and are mostly bound to the crystal in the surface layer. Their TEM images can clearly see the CDs inside the crystals, which are spherical in shape and more uniformly dispersed in the crystals (Fig. 1d-c), and the lattice stripe spacing is 0.5 nm under high transmission electron microscopy, corresponding to the (004) crystal plane of phthalimide (inset of Fig. 1d-c). While a part of the crystals of the powder of G-, Y-, O-SCDs was dissolved to release the CDs from the crystals, and again the CDs were clearly observed by TEM as homogeneous circular spheres (Fig. 1g-f), and the lattice streaks in the high magnification TEM were 0.21 nm, respectively, which was attributed to the (100) face of graphite, and the above proved that the luminescent crystals were phthalimide, as well as the presence of CDs in the crystal.

The structures and elemental compositions of G-, Y-, O-SCDs were characterized by FTIR, XRD and XPS maps. As shown in Fig. 2a, the FTIR spectra of G-, Y-, O-SCDs are essentially identical to those of phthalimide, indicating that the crystals observed in the TEM maps of our institute have a similar chemical structure to that of phthalimide. Among them, 3142 cm−1 is attributed to C-N and the single peak indicates a secondary amine bond. 1746 and 1621 cm−1 peaks are attributed to CO and CC stretching vibrations, respectively. The peak at 1750 cm−1 is attributed to the amide bond. 3200 and 1135 cm−1 are attributed to N-H stretching vibrations and C-H bending vibrations [24,25]. Fig. 2b shows the total XPS elemental maps of G-, Y-, O-SCDs and phthalimide. It can be clearly seen that G-, Y-, O-SCDs and pure phthalimide have C, N and O elements as well. While the N element in the phthalimide is relatively small and not obvious in the figure, the three SCDs are clearly higher in N element than the pure phthalimide due to the influence of CDs in the crystal. In the high-resolution scan (Supporting Information, Fig. S1), all three SCDs and phthalimide have three convolution peaks at 284.7, 286.5, and 288.7 eV in the spectrum of C1s, which are attributed to CC/C-C, C-O/C-N, and CO, respectively. In the XPS high-resolution spectra of O1s, a total of two convolution peaks of 531.8 and 533.6 eV were distinguished, which belong to CO and C-O. Fig. S3 shows the high-resolution spectra of N1s, where 398.9, 399.4 and 400.1 correspond to pyridine nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen and graphite nitrogen [26,27]. The XPS data correspond to the FTIR results. Fig. 2c shows the XRD plot, and the three colors of SCDs and the standard XRD data of phthalimide crystals match well. Both FTIR, XPS and XRD plots are indicating the crystals of the three SCDs as phthalimide. Fig. 2d shows the UV-Vis spectra of the DMSO solutions of G-, Y-, O-SCDs and phthalimide. There are more intense absorption peaks at 259, 292 and 300 nm, which are attributed to π -π * electron leaps in aromatic CC bonds and n-π * electron leaps in aromatic sp2 systems containing CO or C-N bonds. The foregoing characterization demonstrated that the G-, Y-, and O-SCDs have the same functional groups and the formed crystals proved to be o-phenylene dimethyl peptide imides.

The Raman and fluorescence lifetimes spectra were obtained in order to examine the sp2/sp3 structures of G-, Y-, and O-SCDs (Fig. 2e-f). It was shown in Fig. 2e, two peaks appear at 1304.0 and 1604.1 cm-1 for the three SCDs, corresponding to the G (sp2-hybridized carbon) and D (sp3-hybridized carbon) bands, respectively. The IG/ID values of G-SCDs, Y-SCDs and O-SCDs are 2.142, 2.278 and 2.565, respectively. It suggests that higher graphitization and crystallization affect the shift of CDs to longer wavelengths [28]. In addition, steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS-1000) was used to characterize the fluorescence lifetimes of SCDs. The average lifetimes of G-, Y- and O -SCDs were computed by fluorescence decay curve fitting as 3.296, 2.304 and 1.852 ns, respectively (Fig. 2f, Table S2). And their fluorescence lifetimes gradually decreased with the red shift of wavelength, which may be related to the three SCDs with gradually increasing conjugation domains and decreasing band gaps [29].

The optical properties of G-, Y-, and O-SCDs were investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3a–f, the optimal excitation wavelength of G-SCDs is 460 nm and the optimal emission wavelength is 520 nm. the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of Y-SCDs are 460 and 540 nm, respectively, and the maximum emission wavelength has a slight shift with the change of excitation wavelength from 420 to 490 nm, which indicates that their emission has a slight dependence on the excitation wavelength. The best excitation of SCDs is at 370 nm and the emission is at 599 nm, with a very large Stokes shift, which is favorable to resist the self-quenching phenomenon and the background interference. The Figures g-i show the existence of different luminescence centers G-SCDs, Y-SCDs, O-SCDs in the corresponding excitation-emission matrices, taking PL excitation and PL emission spectra along the horizontal dashed line and vertical dashed line, and the results match the excitation and emission peaks of Fig. 3a–f.

The fluorescence stability of the three SCDs was investigated, the changes of their fluorescence intensity continuous irradiation time with 365 nm UV light, at different temperatures, and at prolonged placement were investigated (Fig. S4, Supporting Information). The maximum decay of intensity was 8.5 % when irradiated with a continuous UV source for 8 h, 18.8 % at increasing temperatures up to 90 °C, and 15.8 % after the same prolonged placement for 3 months. Overall, the fluorescence decay rate did not exceed 20 % and had good stability over temperature, UV irradiation and long storage time. Due to the solubility of phthalimide in dilute bases and the good dispersion of CDs in water, the three SCDs are not easily soluble in solvents such as water, ethanol, and methanol, but are readily soluble in alkaline solutions. Considering the specificity of its solid-state fluorescence properties, the main focus is on the solid-state related properties.

In contrast to CDs dispersed in solution, CDs in the aggregated state are usually due to direct π-π interactions or the distance between adjacent CDs is less than R0 and excessive FRET occurs, which in turn leads to ACQ [30,31]. Therefore, providing a certain distance between CDs is a remarkably well-suited solution. The phthalimide crystals loaded by G-SCDs, Y-SCDs and O-SCDs not only act as carriers of CDs but also limit the distance between CDs. PA promotes the formation of amide bonds on the surface of CDs, which increases the surface defects of CDs and makes them have efficient luminescence properties. It all prevents the CDs from getting too close to produce FRET or strong ACQ. Fluorescence red-shift is attributed to the nitrogen doping of CDs that affects the degree of crystallinity, resulting in a change in the conjugation domain [32,33]. In Table S1 for each elemental content ratio, G-SCDs, Y-SCDs and O-SCDs contain an increase in the proportion of elemental N as the UA specific gravity of the feedstock increases, and all three SCDs have significantly elevated elemental N content compared to phthalimide. The experimental data showed that the ratio of PA to UA decreased, i.e., the N nitrogen element of doped CDs in the solvothermal process increased when the UA precursor content increased. Based on the results of the characterization of the relevant structures, the models that CDs have been proposed and the LUMO and HOMO of the models are calculated by Gaussian software using density flooding theory (DFT) ( Fig. 4). The band gaps of G-SCDs, Y-SCDs and O-SCDs are 1.39 eV, 1.12 and 0.71 eV, respectively, which gradually decrease with wavelength redshift, corresponding to the gradually decreasing fluorescence lifetimes of 3.296, 2.304 and 1.852 ns in Fig. 2f. The trend data of the band gap prove that the proposed model of SCDs is reasonable and the obtained data are also reliable.

Benefiting from their tunable color solid-state optical properties and stability, SCDs can be used to prepare color LEDs. 365 nm LED chip was used as the excitation source, and G-SCDs, Y-SCDs and O-SCDs were used as color conversion layers and epoxy resin were completely mixed and painted on the LED chip, which was cured by adding ethylenediamine dropwise to finally obtain three colors of LEDs ( Fig. 5a–c). For the G-, Y- and O-SCDs, the CIE coordinates are (0.38,0.54), (0.44,0.54) and (0.51,0.47), respectively, as shown in Fig. 5d–f. The CRI values were 69.2, 71. 6 and 76.4 for green, yellow and orange LEDs with CCT values of 7912, 6147 and 2485 K, respectively.

It is noteworthy that the O-SCDs powders also exhibit RTP emission when the UV is turned off. By changing the temperature of the hydrothermal process to 140, 160, and 180 °C, the generated SCDs powders are SCDs-140, SCDs-160, and SCDs-180. interestingly, the naked-eye RTP times of SCDs-140 and 160 are around 800 ms, which is 30 ms longer than the delayed RTP of SCDs generated at 180 °C is considerably longer ( Fig. 6d and Movie 1–3). As shown in Fig. 6a, the normalized RTP spectra of SCDs-140, SCDs-160 and SCDs-180 have phosphorescent emission at 575,579,530 nm, respectively. The RTP emission of CDs appears to be less in the spectral range of orange emission. This method also provides a way to obtain the orange RTP. The decay spectra of RTP lifetimes of SCDs are shown in Fig. 6b, and the three RTP SCDs fitting parameters are summarized in Supporting Information Table S3. SCDs-160 has the longest mean decay lifetime of 156.8 ms, followed by SCDs-140 with a phosphorescence lifetime of 122.2 ms and SCDs-180 with a phosphorescence lifetime of 36.4 ms, compared to SCDs-140 and SCDs-160 exhibited a significant reduction.

After a molecule absorbs a photon, the electron may leap from the ground state (S0) to the excited singlet state (Sn), and then reach the lowest vibrational energy level of the first excited singlet state (S1) through processes such as internal conversion (IC) and vibrational relaxation (VR), and the electron leaps radiologically from the lowest vibrational energy level of the S1 state to the ground state, producing fluorescence (FL), and some electrons may leap to the excited triplet state (Tn) through the inter-system scramble (ISC) process, and then continue to reach the lowest vibrational energy level of the T1 state through processes such as IC and VR, and finally leap radiologically back to the S0 state and produce phosphorescence (Phos) (Fig. 6c) [34,35]. In the ISC process of electron jumping from S1 state to T1 state, the spin multiplicity of electron changes, and the original two spin paired electrons are no longer paired, according to the rules of quantum mechanics, the jump between two electrons need the same spin multiplicity, so this jump is generally difficult to occur, called spin-barred jump. It has been shown that spin-orbit coupling (SOC) allows the mixing of wave functions between S and T states, thus making the ISC process possible. The incorporation of heteroatoms such as O, N, and P can promote the n→π * jump, which in turn enhances spin-orbit coupling and helps to pool triplet excitons through the ISC process [36]. Based on the literature, Embedding CDs in a matrix or adding elements such as silica and rigid structures to achieve RTP. It also effectively prevents triplet state quenching and limits the vibration and rotation of molecules.

The large overlap between the phosphorescence excitation spectra of SCDs-140, − 160, − 180 and UV-Vis absorption spectra confirms that the structure corresponding to the absorption band of the UV-Vis spectrum near 300 nm (C-N/CN) is the origin of phosphorescence (Fig. S8). On the one hand, the doping of N elements, which possess lone pair electrons can facilitates n-π * transition and promotes spin-orbit coupling (SOC) effects [33,37]. On the other hand, due to the incorporation of amide carbonyl (CN), abundant new energy level are created and as a result, the energy gap (ΔEST) between the lowest singlet state and the lowest triplet state is drastically reduced, which is about 0.21–0.23 eV according to Eq.1 in Fig. S9. Both of the factors mentioned above promote the effective inter-system crossing from the S to the T state [38,39]. Notably, only the O-SCDs with the highest N content had a weak RTP emission, and the RTP emission of the other two ratios of G-SCDs and Y-SCDs could not be captured by the naked eye. The generated SCDs did not have RTP emission after removing UA from the feedstock RTP emission that pure phthalimide drugs do not possess (Fig. S12), which proved that the nitrogen-containing functional groups had a great influence on the RTP emission. Temperature is also one of the pathways to modulate the phosphor lifetime. Among the three reaction temperatures of 140, 160 and 180 °C, SCDs-160 had the longest phosphorescence lifetime and SCDs-180 had the worst RTP performance. We speculate that increasing the reaction temperature can, to a certain extent, promote reactions such as dehydration to form a more compact structure and promote phosphorescence, while if the temperature continues to increase, it will make the C-N/CN bond carbonize, reduce the center of RTP luminescence and weaken the interparticle hydrogen bonding [40].

Phthalimides containing SCDs play an essential role in the formation of hydrogen bonds in the phosphorescence emission of SCDs-140, 160 and 180. Since hydrogen bonds may be broken when dissolved in polar solvents, solvent systems with different DMF/EA ratios were equipped to investigate the effects. As shown in Fig. S11, SCDs-180 with a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL were prepared with different ratios of mixed solvents, where the RTP emission decreased significantly with the increase of DMF content. It proves the existence of hydrogen bonding. On the one hand, the presence of crystals can increase the distance between SCDs, attenuate the ACQ effect due to particle aggregation. In the crystalline state, the intramolecular motion is restricted by intermolecular interactions such as interparticle hydrogen bonds formed between the lattice and the nitrogen-containing groups on CDs and the externally wrapped crystals, allowing the non-radiative relaxation channels to be inhibited. On the other hand, the dense crystals formed externally can effectively protect the triplet state of SCDs from quenching.

The unique RTP-emitting properties of SCDs-140 and 160 powders render them valuable for data encryption applications. SCDs-160 was used to write the hidden security number "1912" on a background sheet of non-illuminated paper. The powder is mixed with Y-SCDs ink to write 8888. As shown in Fig. 6e and Movie 4, the ink can be clearly observed under UV light as the number 8888. The cryptographic feature "1912", on the other hand, can only be seen with the UV lamp turned off, as it originates from the orange RTP of SCDs.

The tunable solid-emitting SCDs in this study were synthesized by a solvothermal one-step method by adjusting the ratio of UA to phthalimide. Phthalimide acts as a carrier for the CDs, which can provide the distance necessary for the particle to inhibit ACQ. The fluorescence emission of SCDs can be gradually red-shifted from green to orange due to the increase of UA ratio in the precursor. The nitrogen doping ratio of CDs gradually increases, which further increases the conjugated domain area of CDs and finally decreases the band gap of CDs HUMO and LUMO. The energy gaps between the HOMO and LOMO of G-, Y- and O- SCD are 1.39 eV, 1.12 and 0.71 eV, respectively, as calculated by DFT, which also confirms that the fluorescence redshift is attributed to the change of the energy band gap. Phosphorescence properties of CDs can be tuned by adjusting the synthesis temperature. At 160 °C, the phosphorescence lifetime of SCDs-160 is 156.8 ms with an orange phosphorescence emission peak of 579 nm. It furnishes the way to achieve long wavelength phosphorescence. Phosphorescence with an energy band gap in the range of 0.21–0.23 eV, demonstrating an effective transition from the S to the T state. The presence of phthalimide crystals reduces the vibration and rotation of surface functional groups and suppresses the non-radiative channels, while nitrogen-containing functional groups promote the ISC of photons. Based on their excellent solid-state optical properties, green, yellow and orange LEDs with different CIE coordinates can also be prepared. Orange phosphorescence up to about 800 ms can be used to make inks in the field of digital encryption. This work promotes the wide application of multicolor fluorescent SCDs and long-wavelength phosphorescence in fields such as optoelectronic devices and anti-counterfeit encryption.

Fanyong Yan: Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Resources. Chunhui Yi: Investigation, Experiments and data processing, Writing – original draft. Zhonghua Hao: Data curation. Yao Wang: Supervision. Ming Xu: Supervision. Keqing Zhou: Review. Fangyu Shi: Review. Jinxia Xu: Review.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51678409), Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology of China (19JCYBJC19800), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (TD13-5042).

We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University for Transmission Electron Microscope, Cold Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope, X-Ray Diffractometer, Raman spectrometer, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129626.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
